457	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
459	
460	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
461	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
462	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
463	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
464	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
465	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
466	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
467	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
470	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
471	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
472	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
473	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
474	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
475	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
476	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
477	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
478	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
479	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).
482	
483	\begin{lemma}
484	
485	\leanok
486	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
487	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
488	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
489	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
490	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
491	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
492	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
493	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
494	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
495	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
496	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
497	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
498	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
499	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
500	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
501	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
502	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
503	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
504	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
505	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
506	  \[
507	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
508	      \;\;:=\;\;
509	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
510	  \]
511	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
512	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
513	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
514	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
515	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
516	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
517	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	
522	
523	\leanok
524	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
525	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
526	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
527	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
528	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
529	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
530	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
531	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
532	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
533	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
534	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
535	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
536	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
537	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
538	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
539	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
540	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
541	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
542	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
543	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
544	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
545	
546	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
547	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
548	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
549	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
550	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
551	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\begin{lemma}
555	
556	
557	\leanok
558	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
559	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
561	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
562	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
563	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
564	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
565	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
566	  morphism
567	  \[
568	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
569	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
570	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
571	  \]
572	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
573	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
574	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
575	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
576	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
577	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
578	\end{lemma}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
582	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
583	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
584	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
585	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
586	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
587	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
588	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
589	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
590	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
591	  isomorphism}
592	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
593	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
594	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
595	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
596	  therefore extension of scalars along
597	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
598	
599	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction

771	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
772	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
773	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
774	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
775	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
776	  product: the canonical map
777	  \[
778	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
779	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
780	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
781	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
782	  \]
783	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
784	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
785	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
786	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
787	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
788	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
789	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
790	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
791	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
792	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
793	\end{lemma}

3313	  The map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is built (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) by
3314	  transporting the abstract oplax comparison \(\delta\) of the presheaf pullback
3315	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through the sheafification
3316	  reconciliations. Each ingredient is natural: the abstract oplax \(\delta\) is natural
3317	  in both arguments --- this holds for any oplax monoidal functor --- and the sheafification,
3318	  the \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) comparison, and the unit/tensor reconciliation
3319	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) are natural transformations. The asserted square is the
3320	  pasting of these naturality squares, so it commutes; naturality in the two arguments is
3321	  checked separately and then combined, the tensor of the two component morphisms factoring
3322	  as \(f^*a \otimes f^*b\) by bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_Y\).

3324	  The fourth square --- the naturality of \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\)
3325	  (\cref{lem:sheafify_tensor_unit_iso_natural}) --- is the one square that is \emph{not}
3326	  closed by the formal monoidal interchange (whisker-exchange) law. When the comparison is
3327	  expanded into its whisker factors, the whiskers of the sheafification unit \(\eta\) and
3328	  the factors coming from the tensor-of-morphisms \(p \otimes q\) are framed by two
3329	  monoidal structures on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) that agree on the nose but whose
3330	  syntactic mismatch prevents the interchange law from bridging between them.

3332	  principle for presheaf-of-modules morphisms (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom.ext}\)) reduces
3333	  the square to an equality of its components over each open \(U\). Over \(U\) the whiskers
3334	  unfold to honest tensor-of-morphisms maps, and after module-level extensionality
3335	  (\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.hom\_ext}\)) the identity reduces, by \(\mathtt{TensorProduct}\)
3336	  induction, to the sectionwise naturality of \(\eta\) on each pure tensor \(p \otimes q\):
3337	  \[
3338	    p_U \circ (\eta_{M'})_U = (\eta_{M})_U \circ (f^*a)_U,
3339	    \qquad
3340	    q_U \circ (\eta_{N'})_U = (\eta_{N})_U \circ (f^*b)_U,
3341	  \]
3342	  combined bilinearly. Thus the fourth square reduces to the bilinear, sectionwise
3343	  naturality of the sheafification unit \(\eta\), verified one pure tensor at a time.
3344	\end{proof}

3348	   \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\)]

3352	  fourth naturality square of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural} is natural in both
3353	  module arguments. Equivalently: after descending to sections over each open \(U\) and
3354	  passing to the underlying module components, the naturality square is the
3355	  bilinear, sectionwise naturality of the sheafification unit \(\eta\), verified one pure
3356	  tensor \(p \otimes q\) at a time by \(\mathtt{TensorProduct}\) induction.
3357	\end{lemma}

3359	\begin{proof}
3360	  \uses{lem:sheafify_tensor_unit_iso}
3361	  Write \(\Phi\) for the natural transformation whose naturality square (in the two module
3362	  arguments \(M, N\), along morphisms \(a : M \to M'\) and \(b : N \to N'\)) is to be shown
3363	  to commute; \(\Phi\) is assembled from the reconciliation isomorphism
3364	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) (\cref{lem:sheafify_tensor_unit_iso}) and so its
3365	  components are morphisms of presheaves of modules. We close the square in three descents.
3366	
3367	  \emph{Step 1: descend to sections.} A morphism of presheaves of modules is determined by
3368	  its components over each open \(U\): two such morphisms agree as soon as their components
3369	  agree over every \(U\) (the extensionality principle
3370	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom.ext}\)). It therefore suffices to fix an open \(U\) and to
3371	  prove that the two composites around the square agree as \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-linear maps
3372	  out of \((M \otimes_X N)(U)\). Before reading off the comparison componentwise one
3373	  reconciles the carrier spelling: the identity-restriction strip
3374	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsId\_map}\) rewrites the restriction of scalars along the identity
3375	  to the bare module map, so that both legs are presented as honest
3376	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-linear maps with the same source and target.
3377	
3378	  \emph{Step 2: descend to elements.} Two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-linear maps out of the module
3379	  \((M \otimes_X N)(U)\) agree as soon as they agree on every element (the extensionality
3380	  principle \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.hom\_ext}\)). Each composite around the square is
3381	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-linear, hence in particular additive and unit-preserving: both send
3382	  the zero element to zero and respect addition. The module \((M \otimes_X N)(U)\) is
3383	  generated by the pure tensors \(p \otimes q\) with \(p \in M(U)\), \(q \in N(U)\), and an
3384	  element of a tensor product is built from pure tensors by the universal property
3385	  (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct}\) induction): the zero and additivity cases are formal because
3386	  both composites are additive and preserve \(0\). It therefore suffices to check that the
3387	  two composites agree on every pure tensor \(p \otimes q\).
3388	
3389	  \emph{Step 3: the pure-tensor identity is two unit-naturalities.} On a pure tensor
3390	  \(p \otimes q\) the value of the comparison factors as the tensor of its two
3391	  single-argument legs, one in the first module argument and one in the second. The
3392	  pure-tensor equality therefore splits as the conjunction of two independent identities,
3393	  each of which is precisely a naturality square of the sheafification unit
3394	  \(\eta = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}).\mathrm{unit}\)
3395	  (the unit \(\mathbf{1} \Rightarrow (\text{sheafify}) \circ (\text{inclusion})\) of the
3396	  sheafification adjunction), evaluated at the relevant element:
3397	  \[
3398	    (\eta_{M'})_U \circ \big(\text{induced map}\big)_U
3399	      = \big(\text{induced map}\big)_U \circ (\eta_{M})_U
3400	      \quad\text{evaluated at } p,
3401	  \]
3402	  in the first argument, and the analogous identity in the second argument evaluated at
3403	  \(q\). Each of these holds simply because \(\eta\) is a natural transformation, so its
3404	  naturality square commutes by definition. Tensoring the two evaluated identities yields the
3405	  equality of the two composites on the pure tensor \(p \otimes q\); by the bilinearity and
3406	  pure-tensor reduction of Step 2 this extends to all of \((M \otimes_X N)(U)\), and by the
3407	  componentwise reduction of Step 1 it proves that the naturality square of
3408	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) commutes.
3409	\end{proof}

5741	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}
5742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homOfLocalCompat}
5743	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and \(\{U_i\}\) an
5744	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
5745	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
5746	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
5747	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5748	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
5749	\end{lemma}
5750	
5751	\begin{proof}
5752	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
5753	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
5754	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
5755	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
5756	
5757	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
5758	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
5759	  \(\mathcal{H}\) (\(\mathtt{presheafHom}\) of the underlying presheaves of abelian groups
5760	  of \(M\) and \(N\)), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
5761	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
5762	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
5763	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
5764	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
5765	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
5766	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
5767	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
5768	  strictly more bookkeeping.
5769	
5770	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
5771	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}(U_i)\)
5772	  of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section},
5773	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
5774	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
5775	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
5776	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
5777	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
5778	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality condition} of
5779	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
5780	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
5781	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece to be
5782	  built and verified first, as a standalone lemma.
5783	
5784	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
5785	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
5786	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
5787	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
5788	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
5789	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
5790	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
5791	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
5792	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
5793	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
5794	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
5795	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
5796	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
5797	  must be built separately.
5798	
5799	  Of the remaining four sub-steps, three are mechanical and one --- the cocycle bridge
5800	  (a) --- carries the genuine content. We spell it out.
5801	
5802	  \emph{(a) The cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
5803	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\).} The overlap-agreement hypothesis is phrased with
5804	  \emph{heterogeneous} equality (\(\mathtt{HEq}\)) rather than ordinary equality: the two
5805	  double-restrictions \((f_i)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) and \((f_j)|_{U_i \cap U_j}\) reach the
5806	  overlap by two different slice-restriction routes, and the resulting morphisms live in
5807	  types that are propositionally equal but not definitionally equal, so a homogeneous
5808	  equation between them does not even type-check. What \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) asks for,
5809	  by contrast, is an \emph{honest} equality of the two restrictions of the local sections
5810	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) and \(\mathtt{localSection}\,j\) to the overlap, as sections
5811	  of the hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) over \(U_i \sqcap U_j\), i.e.\ an equation in the single
5812	  type \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\). The bridge from one to the other transports the
5813	  heterogeneous agreement through the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) conjugation built into
5814	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) (its sectionwise components and naturality,
5815	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_hom_local_section}). Concretely, restricting
5816	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\) to the overlap evaluates sectionwise to the corresponding
5817	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications,
5818	  and likewise for \(j\); once both sides are transported into the common type
5819	  \(\mathcal{H}(U_i \sqcap U_j)\) along those \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)s, the route-difference
5820	  between the two restriction maps collapses --- any two parallel morphisms in the thin
5821	  poset \((\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}}\) are equal, by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\)
5822	  --- and the heterogeneous agreement becomes the required genuine equality of the two
5823	  transported sections. Thus the \(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition is exactly the assumed
5824	  agreement of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\), recovered after the
5825	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-transport and the \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) identification of
5826	  the comparison maps.
5827	
5828	  The other three sub-steps are mechanical: (b) gluing via
5829	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
5830	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g\) of the underlying presheaves of
5831	  \(M\) and \(N\) --- the
5832	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
5833	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
5834	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
5835	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
5836	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
5837	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
5838	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
5839	
5840	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
5841	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
5842	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
5843	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
5844	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
5845	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
5846	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
5847	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
5848	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
5849	
5850	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
5851	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality condition and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
5852	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
5853	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
5854	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
5855	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
5856	  naturality, built as a standalone verified lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
5857	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
5858	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the stalk-tensor commutation.
5859	\end{proof}

5862	[How the dual infrastructure discharges the \(\otimes\)-inverse]
5863	  \label{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}
5864	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial,
5865	        lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict,
5866	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5867	  Once \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom,def:presheaf_dual,%
5868	  lem:internal_hom_isSheaf,lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} are available,
5869	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is discharged with no further missing object:
5870	  take \(L^{-1} := \mathtt{dual}\,L\) (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}), which is a line
5871	  bundle by \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}. The global contraction \(\varepsilon_L
5872	  \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{not} obtained by sheafifying
5873	  the presheaf-level evaluation; it is instead built by \emph{gluing the canonical local
5874	  contraction morphisms}. On each trivialising open \(U\), the
5875	  restriction-compatibility iso \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} carries
5876	  \((L \otimes_X L^{-1})|_U\) to \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U\), and the
5877	  canonical local contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\)
5878	  of the free rank-one module with its dual is the left unitor
5879	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, an isomorphism. These local contractions are canonical,
5880	  hence agree on overlaps, and so glue --- by the A-bridge
5881	  \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat} --- to a single global morphism
5882	  \(\varepsilon_L \colon L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\). Since ``being an
5883	  isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) restricts to an isomorphism on
5884	  each \(U\), it is a global isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim}
5885	  \mathcal{O}_X\) by the B-connector \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}.
5886	
5887	  This descent construction deliberately avoids the forbidden
5888	  ``sheafify-the-evaluation'' route --- which would re-enter the abandoned
5889	  tensor-stalk / sheafification-unit whiskering commutation --- and computes no tensor
5890	  stalk anywhere. It is the realization the ``infrastructure-blocked'' note in
5891	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} points to.
5892	\end{remark}

5564	[Dual of the structure sheaf]
5565	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
5566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
5567	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5568	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5569	  structure sheaf itself,
5570	  \[
5571	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5572	  \]
5573	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5574	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5575	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5576	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5577	\end{lemma}
5578	
5579	\begin{proof}
5580	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
5581	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5582	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5583	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5584	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5585	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5586	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5587	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5588	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5589	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5590	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5591	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
5592	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87

8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,

18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);

59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a

50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44

46	
47	\begin{theorem}

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:

10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.

43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.

20	\section{Setup and motivation}

31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.

61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
